title: SuperBEST v3 Cost Analysis — Biology Block 4: Cooperative Binding Models

routing_table.note: Parameters are free terminals (cost 0). Variables are free terminals (cost 0).

equations.name: Hill equation
equations.description: General Hill equation with Hill coefficient n (integer >= 1). Vmax, K, n are parameters.
equations.dominant_term: Two pow(x,n) calls at cost 3 each = 6 total. Fixed overhead = 9.
notes: This is purely algebraic — no exp or ln needed.
pow([S],n) can share the computed value in numerator and denominator sum, so only 2 distinct pow calls.
If n=1 (Michaelis-Menten): pow degenerates to identity (cost 0 each), total drops to 15 - 6 = 9.
Cost is independent of n because pow(x,n) costs 3 regardless of n value.

equations.name: Hill equation — fractional saturation
equations.description: Fractional saturation form. Kd = dissociation constant, [L] = ligand concentration. Vmax removed (normalised to 1).

operations.note: [L]^n — used in both numerator and denominator

notes: Kd does NOT need a pow: Kd is a parameter, it absorbs K^n directly (Kd already is K^n in the dissociation sense).
Only one pow call needed — [L]^n shared between numerator and denominator sum.
This is the minimal Hill form; Eq 1 adds Vmax scaling (+5 cost).
n=1 special case: pow degenerates, cost drops to 10 - 3 = 7.

equations.name: Hill equation — linearized (Hill plot)
equations.description: Linearized Hill equation. log10 = ln / ln(10) so log10(x) = ln(x) * recip(ln(10)). ln(10) is a constant parameter.

log10_cost_analysis.log10(x): ln(x) [cost 1] * recip(ln10_const) [cost 2] * mul [cost 2] = 5, BUT ln10 is a fixed parameter so recip(ln10) is also a parameter = cost 0. Thus log10(x) = ln(x) * param = ln[1] + mul[2] = 3.
log10_cost_analysis.simplified: log10(x) costs 3 (one ln + one mul by constant 1/ln10)

operations.note: theta itself must be computed; if theta is an input variable it is free
log of odds — part of log10
converts ln to log10; 1/ln10 is a free parameter
n * log10([S]); n is a free parameter
log10(K) — K is a parameter, so ln(K)/ln(10) can be folded into a single parameter constant; cost 0
final subtraction; log10(K) absorbed into parameter

equations.cost_formula: sub(1-theta)[2] + recip[2] + mul(odds)[2] + ln(odds)[1] + mul(to_log10)[2] + ln([S])[1] + mul(to_log10)[2] + mul(n*)[2] + sub(final)[2] = 16
notes: ln(K) is absorbed into a free parameter (log10(K) = constant), saving 3 cost.
1/ln(10) is a free parameter constant — no recip node needed at runtime.
The dominant cost is the log10 conversions (2 ln calls + 2 mul calls = 6) plus the odds ratio computation (2+2=4).
No pow() node needed — the linearized form replaces [S]^n with n*log([S]).
This is cheaper than the algebraic form when n is large: algebraic form costs 15 fixed; linearized costs 16 fixed but avoids pow(x,n).
For n >= 2 the algebraic form is cheaper (15 vs 16). For large n the algebraic form stays at 15 while linearized stays at 16 — linearized never wins on cost alone.
Linearized form is used for graphical analysis (Hill plot), not cost minimization.

equations.name: Two-site binding (non-cooperative)
equations.description: Two sequential binding sites with microscopic constants K1, K2. Factor of 2 absorbed into parameters where possible.

parameter_folding.B: K1*K2 — product is a single free parameter (call it K12)

operations.note: multiply by 2 — OR absorb 2 into parameter K1,K12 redefinition

equations.cost_formula: pow[3] + mul[2] + mul[2] + add[3] + mul[2] + add[3] + add[3] + mul[2] + recip[2] + mul[2] = 24
equations.optimization_note: The factor of 2 in denominator can be absorbed into parameter redefinitions (K1' = K1/2, K12' = K12/2), eliminating one mul node. Also, the two mul(K_param, [L]^2) in numerator and denominator can be merged if coefficient ratio is absorbed into a single parameter. Minimum: 22.
notes: pow([L],2) = 3 is the dominant single node. Only one pow call since [L]^2 is reused.
The coefficient 2 in numerator (2*K1*K2) is absorbed into parameter K12_2 = 2*K12, saving one mul node.
Three add nodes are unavoidable — the polynomial has three distinct terms in the denominator.
This is considerably more expensive than the n=2 Hill form (cost 10) because it tracks two separate binding constants K1 and K2.
The Hill form is a collapsed version of this that assumes K_eff = (K1*K2)^(1/2) and uses only one pow.

equations.name: MWC model (Monod-Wyman-Changeux) — n=2 case

substitutions.alpha: [L]/KR  — KR is a free parameter, so alpha = [L]*recip(KR)
substitutions.c: KR/KT — free parameter (ratio of two constants)
substitutions.alpha_c: alpha * c = [L]*c/KR — or define beta = [L]/KT as free expression

alpha_computation.note: recip(KR) is a fixed parameter = 0 cost; mul costs 2

alpha_c_computation.note: c = KR/KT is a free parameter

num_term2_inner.note: L is free parameter; mul(L, alpha_c)

notes: n=2 is the explicit hemoglobin-like case (two subunit MWC).
The two pow(x,2) calls each cost 3, total 6 for the pow nodes.
Four add nodes (cost 3 each = 12) dominate the overhead.
alpha and alpha*c can share the mul(alpha,c) result — no redundant computation.
The (1+alpha) and (1+alpha*c) intermediate values are each used twice (in pow and in numerator mul), so no repeat computation is needed.

For general n: replace pow(x,2) with pow(x,n) [still cost 3 each] and add (1+alpha)^(n-1) and (1+alpha_c)^(n-1) separately. General-n cost = 34 + 2*pow_extra = 34 (pow costs are the same regardless of n). The n-1 exponent terms require additional pow calls: +3 each => general-n MWC = 34 + 6 = 40.
General n MWC cost breakdown: same 14 ops but pow(x,n) and pow(x,n-1) needed separately — adds 2 more pow nodes = +6 => 40 total.
equations.general_n_note: For general n: need pow(x,n) and pow(x,n-1) for both (1+alpha) and (1+alpha*c) branches — 4 pow nodes vs 2 for n=2. Extra cost: +6. Total: 40.

equations.name: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation (Hill form)
equations.description: Hill equation applied to hemoglobin-O2 binding. n ≈ 2.7 (non-integer). P50 = half-saturation pressure.
equations.non_integer_n_note: n ≈ 2.7 is non-integer. In SuperBEST v3, pow(x, n) costs 3 regardless of whether n is integer or real — n is treated as a free parameter terminal.

operations.note: pO2^n; n=2.7 is a free parameter — cost 3
P50^n; P50 is a parameter, n is a parameter — pow of two parameters costs 3

notes: Structurally identical to Eq 2 (fractional saturation Hill) but with P50 as a named parameter for the half-saturation point.
P50^n: since P50 is a fixed parameter, pow(P50,n) with fixed n could be collapsed to a single parameter at evaluation time. However, if n is a trainable parameter, pow(P50,n) costs 3.
If P50^n is treated as a single fused parameter constant, cost drops to 10 (same as Eq 2).
Non-integer n (2.7) does NOT change the SuperBEST node cost — pow(x,n) costs 3 for any n.
For the biologically relevant n=2.7, this is the standard clinical approximation formula.
Minimum cost (P50^n fused to param): 10. Standard cost (both pow active): 13.

cross_cutting_observations: All Hill-family equations are purely algebraic — no exp() or ln() nodes except in the linearized (log-log) form.
pow(x,n) at cost 3 is the dominant atomic operation in all cooperative binding models.
The linearized Hill form (Eq 3) replaces pow with ln — trading cost-3 pow nodes for cost-1 ln nodes, but requires extra mul nodes for log10 conversion, resulting in comparable total cost.
The minimal Hill form (Eq 2, fractional saturation) achieves cost 10 with a single pow node — the cheapest cooperative binding expression.
MWC (Eq 5) is the most expensive due to its two-branch allosteric structure requiring parallel pow chains and multiple add nodes.
Two-site non-cooperative binding (Eq 4) is cheaper than MWC but more expensive than the collapsed Hill form, reflecting the trade-off between mechanistic detail and representational economy.

For integer n, pow(x,n) can in principle be computed by repeated multiplication: n-1 mul nodes at cost 2 each. For n=2: 1 mul = cost 2 (cheaper than pow cost 3). For n=3: 2 muls = cost 4 > pow cost 3. So for n=2 only, manual squaring beats the pow primitive.
n=2 optimization: replace pow(x,2) with mul(x,x) [cost 2 vs 3]. Equations with n=2 save 1 cost per pow node replaced.

eq1_n2.note: Both pow([S],2) and pow(K,2) replaced by mul

eq5_n2.note: Both pow(1+alpha,2) and pow(1+alpha_c,2) replaced by mul

eq6_n_noninteger.note: n=2.7 is non-integer — cannot use repeated mul; pow primitive required
